content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 31, 1990


Dianna M. Higdon
Norco City Clerk,
City Hall
P.O. Box 428
Norco, CA  91760

Re:  Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-90-685

Dear Ms. Higdon:

     This is in response to your November 2, 1990, letter
requesting advice concerning the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act ("Act").1  Your request is one for
general assistance. Accordingly, we are treating it as a request
for informal assistance.2/

QUESTION

     You have asked the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") for advice regarding specific positions within the
City of Norco (the "city") and whether these positions are to be
considered public officials who manage public investments.

CONCLUSION

     Pursuant to Section 82011, the code reviewing body for a
single city agency is the city council.  Single county agencies
report to their board of supervisors.  All multi-county
jurisdictional agencies fall under the authority of the Fair
Political Practices Commission.  As the Commission is not your
agency's code reviewing body, we are unable to assist you

_______________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Sect 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-685
Page 2


specifically in the determinations of your specifically listed
employees.

At this point in time, we are instructing agencies to
continue including filers in or their conflict of interest codes
until such time as the Commission has clarified the scope of
application of the term "other public officials who manage public
investments." However, positions now specifically listed in
Section.87200, e.g., city and county treasurers, should be filing
as Article 2 filers, utilizing the Form 721.

FACTS

You received information regarding the inclusion of "public
officials who manage public investments" into the list of Article
2 Filers during your attendance at a recent Commission filing
officers' workshop. You are questioning whether the positions of
Parks and Recreation Director, Economic Development Director,
Housing Specialist, Community Services Commission member, Economic
Development Advisory Commission member and management positions
within the parks and recreation district would qualify as "public
officials who manage public investments" based upon the individual
duties applicable to their position.

ANALYSIS

Effective January 1, 1990, Section 87200 of the Government
Code was amended to read:

This article is applicable to elected state officers,
judges and commissioners of courts of the judicial branch of
government, members of the Public Utilities Commission,
members of the Senate Energy Resources Conservation and
Development Commission, members of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, members of the California Coastal
Commission, members of planning commissions, members of the
board of supervisors, district attorneys, county counsels,
county treasurers, and chief administrative officers of
counties, mayors, city managers, city attorneys, city
treasurers, chief administrative officers and members of city
councils of cities, and other public officials who manage
public investments, and to candidates for any of these
offices at any election.

Section 87200, emphasis added.

The Commission is currently considering regulations to
clarify the term "other public officials who manage public
investments." At the present time our interim advice is as
follows:

1. Public officials who are city or county treasurers
must file a Form 721 statement of economic interests.

Our File No. I-90-685
Page 3


     2.     Persons who are "designated employees" in their
agency's conflict-of-interest code may continue to file a
Form 730 statement of economic interests with their filing
officer.

     3.     Persons who are assuming a designated position may
file a Form 730 statement of economic interests.

     4.     Persons who have determined that they manage public
investments, but who are not designated in their agency's
conflict-of-interest code, should file a Form 721 statement
of economic interests.

     5.     Persons not designated in an agency's conflict-of-
interest code and who are uncertain about their status as
public investment managers under Government Code Section
87200 may defer filing statements of economic interests
pending adoption of regulations clarifying the term "public
officials who manage public investments."

     I have placed your name on a Commission mailing list in order
that all updates to these regulations may be forwarded to you for
your perusal.  If you should have any questions regarding this
letter, please do not hesitate to contact our office at (916) 322-
5901.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel


                                   [signature]
                                   By:  Cheryl A. Hoff
                                        Staff Services Analyst
                                        Legal Division

SH:CAH

Enclosures

sections.analysis:
Effective January 1, 1990, Section 87200 of the Government Code was amended to read:

This article is applicable to elected state officers, judges and commissioners of courts of the judicial branch of government, members of the Public Utilities Commission, members of the Senate Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission, members of the Fair Political Practices Commission, members of the California Coastal Commission, members of planning commissions, members of the board of supervisors, district attorneys, county counsels, county treasurers, and chief administrative officers of counties, mayors, city managers, city attorneys, city treasurers, chief administrative officers and members of city councils of cities, and other public officials who manage public investments, and to candidates for any of these offices at any election.

Section 87200, emphasis added.

The Commission is currently considering regulations to clarify the term "other public officials who manage public investments." At the present time our interim advice is as follows:

1. Public officials who are city or county treasurers must file a Form 721 statement of economic interests.

2. Persons who are "designated employees" in their agency's conflict-of-interest code may continue to file a Form 730 statement of economic interests with their filing officer.

3. Persons who are assuming a designated position may file a Form 730 statement of economic interests.

4. Persons who have determined that they manage public investments, but who are not designated in their agency's conflict-of-interest code, should file a Form 721 statement of economic interests.

5. Persons not designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code and who are uncertain about their status as public investment managers under Government Code Section 87200 may defer filing statements of economic interests pending adoption of regulations clarifying the term "public officials who manage public investments."

I have placed your name on a Commission mailing list in order that all updates to these regulations may be forwarded to you for your perusal. If you should have any questions regarding this letter, please do not hesitate to contact our office at (916) 322-5901.
